speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 24th, 1996. The survivor is Alex Chevion, whose name at birth was Alex Fessel. The interviewer is Ruth Meyer. The interview was taking place in Livingston, New Jersey, in the United States, and it will be conducted in English. Hi. This is Ruth Meyer, M-E-Y-E-R. Today is September 24th, 1996. We are with Mr. Alex Chevion in-- in Livingston, New Jersey, in the United States. The interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Chevion, I'm going to start by asking you to tell me your name, and spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Alex Chevion, C-H-E-V-I-O-N. My name at birth was Fessel, F-E-S-S-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name at birth, was it Alex?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex Zisze, Alexander Zisze.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Zisze?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-I-S-Z-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what was the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Date of my birth was July 21st, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that makes you now how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: 72 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. The city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city is Tarnow in Poland, formerly Austrio-Hungary, or Galicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were born--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your father's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Chune C-H-U-N-E. The Hebrew spelling is Elchanan, E-L-C-H-A-N-A-N, Fessel, F-E-S-S-E-L, later Chevion, C-H-E-V-I-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately when was your father born, and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born in 1895 in Golice. And he passed away October 6th, 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kiryat Haim in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother, her name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, her name at birth was Miriam Wilk, W-I-L-K. She was born in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first, she passed away in 1952. And she was 50 years old at that time, so she was born in 1902.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Zabno, Z-A-B-N-O, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she when she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in Kiryat Motzkin in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's also hear about any brothers and sisters, names.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a brother. His name was Peretz Fessel. He lived-- he arrived in the United States in 1950 and he passed away in 1967.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tarnow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, her name is Chava Gilad, G-I-L-A-D. She lives now in Israel, in Kiryat Motzkin. She was born in Tarnow in 1927. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life in Tarnow before things started to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a student. I was growing up, and family was well-off. Life was pleasant, sweet. My mother loved me. And life was great. I had friends. Everything was just the way you would wish for a child to be. I was doing well in school, so studies were not a problem. I enjoyed it. I played soccer occasionally, run away-- run around with the kids, until 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your school. What was the name of it and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- the school-- the school was called Yavneh. It was a parochial school which stressed secular education, but also extensive Hebrew education and religious studies, so all the way through the Talmud, not only the Bible, the Pentateuch, the Prophets and all that, but including the Talmud.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a strictly Jewish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: A strictly Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, that was the grade school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: For high school, I entered another Hebrew school, which was secular. Hebrew was also taught. But the religious emphasis was much smaller. It was more kind of 'modern,' so to speak, quote, unquote.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your childhood at home. Tell me about the house you lived in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived in a house which was on the-- on the street [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a relatively large house. We-- the children had a separate bedroom. There was a large dining room. Every once in a while my parents had big parties in the dining room. Lots of people would be invited and so forth. My bar mitzvah was celebrated in our own house. Somebody-- a caterer came and cooked and baked, all in the house, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about-- tell me about that. Tell me about your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had what they call a drosha, which is a-- like a sermon sort of. But it was more a-- a discussion of certain aspects of the Talmud. In other words, I would ask a certain question and then I would have to resolve this question, which was a very difficult question. And I would have to resolve that by citing passages from the Talmud, Prophets, and so forth and so forth. And it was a long sermon. It probably lasted like two hours. And later, the people would comment on it, you know, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the sermon, no. It was part of the celebration in the house. The synagogue was mostly reading of the Torah. You would be called to read from the Torah, you know, and very much like bar mitzvahs are celebrated here, I would say. But the sermon and the meal and the festivities were later in the house. The sermon was a long sermon. It was-- probably lasted an hour or more, that I as a young boy at the age of 13, you know, had to prepare and memorize and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- that was tough. I remember that. That was tough. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, was my father's friends, business associates, neighbors, and family. And that included a large crowd, a large crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the number. But, you know, it was a very large living room and a large bedroom and the beds were moved out of the bedroom. And the two rooms really were full of people. It was packed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about the house. In Poland in the 1920s, did you have-- how did you know heat the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our house, we had gas heat. And we had gas heat in the bathroom so that the water would be heated, you know. You could take a bath with hot water, a shower with hot water. We had electricity. We had practically all the modern amenities that you would have today. We also had a radio, which in those days was a rarity, you know. And I still remember listening to Hitler's speeches over that radio. That was, you know, shocking to hear the way he was speaking right there and then. And nobody imagined the consequences which would follow from those speeches, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you remember starting to hear those speeches?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we started to hear his speeches in 1933 after he came to power, not before he came to power, just about that time. And the neighbors would sometimes come, knowing that he's going to give a speech. That was talked about, that he will give that speech and so forth. And as the years went by, later during Kristallnacht and so forth, you know, we heard over the radio more and more about Hitler and what's going to happen and what was happening in Germany. Also, the German Jews of Polish extraction were expelled suddenly in 1938. And they arrived in our town. And there was committees organized to help them. And we came in contact with them as a result of those committees. And I had some friends, you know. And we heard stories from them. But of course, this was such a far cry from what happened later that nobody even remotely imagined that what later happened would happen, even though there was so much brutality during Kristallnacht. And the subsequent expulsion of the Polish Jews was-- was-- just overnight he issued an edict. And anybody-- anybody whose parents were born in Poland, he and his family, even the children were already born in Germany, were just thrown over the border, just like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some of them came to your town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of them came to Tarnow. And Jews in Tarnow organized committees to help them. And my parents were active in those committees. And mostly, it was to find housing for them. And we did find-- all of them, somehow we found accommodations for them. And we were supposed to befriend them. And I befriended some boys, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back a little bit more to your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and to the-- the Jewish community in Tarnow. First of all, tell me about what your father did for a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father owned a factory which was making tar-paper and chemical products, mostly for the preservation of wood and things like that. And he was very busy. But he was also very active in the community. In particular, he was chairman of the board for the school which I went, and really was the biggest supporter of that school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then he was also on the committee which was providing coal for poor people to heat during the winter. I have a book which was published by the Jews of Tarnow after the war about Tarnow and the community and all that. I could maybe show it to your if you're interested. It's a very-- that's thick a book. It-- it really documents the life, the activities of that town. My father is mentioned in the book many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me a little bit more about what it was like growing up in Tarnow as a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a Jew. Well, we were relatively large community, so we had practically-- we had a Jewish club for ping pong. We had a Jewish sports club for soccer. And we had a lot of activities, lots of friends. We did not come much in contact with the Gentile community in Poland, for a number of reasons, which I can go into later if you wish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- so we were sort of a self-contained community very much. But we had everything, you know? We had good schools. And I-- I went to both of them. We had lots of activities. And by and large, it was a happy community, I have to say, although there were many poor people too. That's-- not-- not to forget about that. But Tarnow was-- was the center for garment industry, which supplied clothes for-- ready-made clothes for all of Poland, you know, and also had other industries, like my father's and others. And was a lot-- a very strong Zionist movement. Most-- most Jews in Tarnow with a few exceptions were very strongly Zionist, committed to Israel. My two aunts went to Israel. My uncle went to Israel 1920, very early. As a matter of fact, my great-grandfather, after whom I am named, went to visit in 1870, you know. But that was not so much for Zionistic reasons as for religious reasons. And he lived in Tsfat and died there, was buried there. But then my uncle who was already a Zionist went to Israel in 1920s. I have two aunts who went to Israel, joined a kibbutz. And if they would come to visit, you know, it would be a real interesting affair. People would come to listen to their stories and so forth. So Zionism played a big part in the community. My father went to Israel on a tour in 1932 and he came back. And he lectured so many times and I would go with him, because people were so anxious to hear what Israel-- about Israel and so forth. In those days, people didn't have television, you have to remember, or radio, or even newspapers which would write about Israel, so it was very much word of mouth, somebody like that [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever think about taking the family, your family, and moving to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- we was all talking about it all the time, but you know, like probably next year or some time, or-- but not immediately, you know? And then, of course, the war broke out and was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you would discuss why you were insulated from the non-Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were not so much insulated, but anti-Semitism was very strong in Poland. And it was at various level. It was a little bit different with the so-called Polish intellectuals as it was with plain people. There was a large number of very uneducated people in Poland and so forth. So the anti-Semitism might be exhibited in different ways. But there was very strong anti-Semitism throughout the Polish society.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I felt it in various ways. Every once in a while, I remember, a brick was thrown at me and I had the-- you know, damaged something there for a while that I had to-- and I ultimately recovered. I tried not to find myself go walking alone in a non-Jewish neighborhood. That would have been dangerous, likely to be attacked and so forth, you know. So-- and as time went on, you heard also through the newspapers the Poles were openly advocating that the Jews be expelled from Poland. Not-- it wasn't the government official line, but there were very strong organizations in Poland, which had access to the media, you know, and to the newspapers. And they would announce publicly that Poland has a big problem and the Jews represent that problem and the only solution to that problem is like sending them to Madagascar or something like that. And then there was an organized boycott of Jewish shops, say like in the '30s, the later '30s. It was official. Like even in the town of Tarnow was an organized boycott of Jewish shops and so forth. And as a matter of fact, my father at one point hired the former major of the army, retired and so forth, to represent him, because he got a big contract from the air force for a base which they were building. He was supplying the chemicals to insulate the roofs and so forth. And he hired this major, because he realized that there was this anti-Semitism towards him personally, you know. So it was felt in various ways, but it definitely existed there. So we didn't have as children or youths growing up, young boys, we hardly had any contact with-- with Polish Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about religious observances in your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: The home was observant, religious, strict kosher service. My father was sort of-- he didn't wear a beard and he didn't wear his Tzitzit, which is called the fringes, you know, clothes with fringes. He didn't wear it outside, while others did. And I would say half the population in Tarnow were very strict Orthodox Jews. Today they would be called Hasidic. But they would wear their fringes outwardly, because that supposedly is the command of the Torah, that you should wear them in a way where everybody can see them. They would be observing everything to the minutest detail. Nowhere would males and females pray together in the same room. Females would always be separate and so forth. Women would have to wear long dress. Even young girls would have to wear long sleeves, modest dresses buttoned up all the way top, and legs not exposed and so forth and so forth. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, everybody went to a synagogue, even the non-observant and the non-religious. Nobody would dare think not to go. But on an average Sabbath, I would say probably 70%, something like that I'm guessing, certainly more than half of the Jews would go to synagogue to pray.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family go?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family went every Saturday to synagogue. And I followed with my father to synagogue, every Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember the prayers. I remember the-- the camaraderie. During the prayers, every-- you had to know where. Certain passages you would stop talking. But there were certain passages which were considered less important and you would allow yourself to talk. So it was sort of also a way of socializing during prayers, you know. And when I was very young, I would play in the yard there outside the synagogue. Later, of course, I wouldn't, after bar mitzvah in particular. So, you know, you were expected to-- to follow the prayers most of the time. And there was-- twice a week, I would also go to a synagogue to get a lesson in the Talmud from somebody, a selected rabbi who would teach the Talmud. So basically, that's-- it was strictly observant, much more than you say you find today in Europe or in America or throughout the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the celebrations of Shabbos and holidays in your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Shabbos would start, of course, with my mother lighting the candles. And then me and my father and my brother would go to the synagogue. When we would come back from the synagogue, my father would make kiddush. And then throughout the meal, we would sing occasional songs. It was not just eating the meal. It was eating and stopping and singing certain songs. One of the songs which I remember was primarily to glorify the role of the woman, my mother, you know. There's a special song like that called 'Eishet Chayil.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you sing a small part of it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I'm not a good singer.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know, but just--

speaker: interviewee
text: But basically what it says is 'Eishet Chayil' means who is lucky the man who finds the woman who is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the great accomplisher, in other words, that the woman's role in the family is very crucial and-- and she's more-- a treasure more than pearls. That's more or less the song. It's taken out of the-- of the part of the Bible, which is called [NON-ENGLISH], which is ascribed to King Solomon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me a little bit more about the rest of Shabbos in your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during Shabbos, of course, no work was done. It was truly a day of rest. But in particular what I remember during Shabbos, that after the meal on Saturday, my father would take me on his knee and I would tell him what I learned from the Talmud and from the Tanakh, which is the Prophets and the Torah, this particular week. And I was looking forwards to it very much. First of all, it was an opportunity to show off before him. And this was sweet. I still remember that. And then my mother would buy sweets, chocolates, you know, the best, which during the week we were not supposed to eat, for various reasons, you know, the teeth would go wrong and all that. But after this sort of examination, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there will be all those sweets, [LAUGHS] a sort of a reward for that and all that. And-- but primarily it wasn't the sweets. It was really an opportunity to show off before my father, which meant an awful lot to me. And he was trying to be critical and ask me questions, sort of difficult questions. And I had to withstand this test, so it was challenging also. It wasn't only-- and every once in a while, if I didn't live up to the expectations, he would show me that I didn't live up to the expectations. So having passed it was-- was very fulfilling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the observance of the holidays. And who was at your house? Before we go on, who was at your house for Shabbos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for Shabbos--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there ever any guests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always. Primarily the family. However, there was the custom in the synagogue that if a man arrived from another town or for whatever reason, the shamas, which was like a sexton, you know, he would go around and ask people to take him on for Sabbath for a Sabbath meal. And because there was more than one, most of the time my father would end up taking somebody home. Also, we had-- there were young boys who would come to Tarnow to study in the yeshiva. And they would be assigned to homes for guests to eat for the whole week, one meal. So we had those also occasionally, our share, whatever the community asked us to-- to join and do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So very often-- I wouldn't say always, but very often we had a guest for Friday night-- Friday night's meal, not for Saturday for some reason I don't remember. But Friday nights mean there was a guest in the house. Otherwise, it was mostly our family. And occasionally, there would be visits from family from other towns who would come from Gorlice and my father's family or-- you know. What about Sukkos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, Sukkos, we-- we made our own sukkah. And I remember that, because this was great joy for the children. You used to prepare all kinds of ornaments, eggs with colored paper cutouts and various thing, and decorate. The sukkah was supposed to be decorated. And it was the decoration of the sukkah which you showed off to your neighbors and friends and family. You would invite them, come to see our sukkah. And so we worked on those decorations many weeks and it was great fun. I enjoyed that very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you use the sukkah for celebration during the holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the holiday, we ate in the sukkah. It wasn't easy, you know, because the sukkah wasn't very large. But we'd squeeze in and eat there. And the meals were brought inside the sukkah and-- We used the balcony, which was fairly large, but not-- of course, limited, because it's a balcony. We built the sukkah on that balcony.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Simchas Torah in Tarnow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simchas Torah was great fun, because that was mostly celebrated in the synagogues, but the emphasis was on children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] sort of like-- well, it was a paper [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] placard with a painting and it had a little door which was opening. And inside the door were pictures of the Torah. And on top there was an apple with a candle. And I was wearing that as a child, very proud. But mostly it was the dancing. There was a lot of dancing in the synagogue, again and again dancing, but with a lot of ecstasy, not just, you know, barely dancing. People were really letting themselves loose in the way they were stomping their feet dancing and singing and-- you know. So it was lots of joy for a child. There was really a lot-- a lot of happiness.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the other holidays? Tell me what you remember about Pesach in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Pesach was-- probably I remember most, because Pesach was celebrated, you know, at home. My father had a white shirt. And we would move up the bed at the front of the table, because according to tradition he is supposed to lean back. And of-- of course, asking of the four questions as a child-- and I was the youngest son, so that was very, very enjoyable. And I felt important, you know. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was something about Pesach-- I'm trying to reach why it was so-- well, the story of Pesach was so fascinating. It was not only the asking of the questions, but I understood at that point every word that I was reading in the Haggadah. And the story of the Haggadah, you know, with-- with the slavery in Egypt and being redeemed from that slavery and Joseph sending-- selling his brothers to, you know-- to these merchants who came by, and then Joseph ending up in Egypt and advising Pharoah, this is fascinating story. It's probably the greatest literature I've ever read, after I've since then read so many books. So that is really fascinating. And maybe that's why Passover meant so much. And of course, you ate only matzoh, which was a little different than other foods. And-- and the house had to be cleaned, swept from everything. And dishes had to be different dishes. My mother had two sets of dishes. So for Passover all dishes were swept clean and hidden away, and new dishes would come up and all this, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did all of that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother. And she had help. She had-- she had a matter of fact two maids. One was a Jewish maid, whom she trusted with the cooking and all that, because she knew how to prepare kosher. And one was a Gentile maid that did the work mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had people working in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Full-time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Full-time. Two. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit yet about Hanukkah. Do you remember Hanukkah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember Hanukkah because Hanukkah was a holiday where we played a lot of games. And we're not supposed to play cards all year long, except on Hanukkah you got permission to play cards. But of course, the-- the game with dreidel was the most peculiar and special for Hannukah, where this is a top that you spin with your fingers. And depending on the letter that falls down, you win money or lose money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So game-- Hannukah was great in that sense, that we played games with a large crowd. Families, friends would come and we all would play dreidel together. Do you remember getting gifts, like here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much gifts, but the winnings were mine. The winnings were mine. What I remember more as gifts I would get for Passover, a lot of clothes, brand new clothes, shoes, and so forth. And of course, I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop here to change tapes.
